skill.name: pdf
skill.description: PDF workflow guidance for extracting text and tables, filling forms, merging and splitting documents, creating reports, managing metadata, adding annotations, and using OCR when needed.
skill.summary: Work with PDF extraction, generation, forms, metadata, OCR, and document assembly.
skill.author: AutumnsGrove

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, filesystem, network, and keyword patterns across documentation and examples. Most are false positives from Markdown install commands, normal PDF file writes, dependency URLs, or substring matches, but one example uses eval() on CSV data. No prompt injection text or confirmed malicious intent was found, so publication is acceptable with a warning.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe eval() in invoice CSV example
medium_findings.description: The batch invoice example parses row data with eval(row['items']). If a user copies this pattern and processes untrusted CSV input, arbitrary Python expressions could execute. This is a real unsafe coding pattern in an example, although it is not evidence of malicious skill intent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line directly calls eval() on CSV-derived data. The surrounding example is documentation, which lowers exploitability but not the unsafe pattern confidence.

low_findings.title: External command findings are mostly documentation examples
low_findings.description: The shell and backtick detections point to Markdown installation snippets and PDF workflow examples, including pip, brew, and helper-script usage. They are expected for a PDF automation skill and do not show command construction from untrusted input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced Markdown examples or package installation guidance. No evidence found of hidden command execution logic.

low_findings.title: Filesystem operations are expected PDF outputs
low_findings.description: The helper script writes generated PDFs, CSV exports, images, metadata updates, and temporary files to user-specified paths. This is normal behavior for a PDF manipulation utility, but users should avoid processing untrusted paths without validation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code performs ordinary document output and temporary-file handling. Risk remains limited to caller-controlled path handling, not malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Network and sudo findings are dependency installation guidance
low_findings.description: The detected URLs and sudo commands appear in operating system dependency instructions for poppler and tesseract. They do not indicate runtime network access, data exfiltration, or unauthorized privilege escalation by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and URLs are clearly labeled install instructions. No evidence found that the helper script contacts these URLs or escalates privileges.

low_findings.title: Keyword blocker findings are substring false positives
low_findings.description: Weak-crypto, C2, reconnaissance, and Windows SAM detections match benign PDF vocabulary such as description, security, language codes, and section headings. The reviewed lines do not contain credential theft, command-and-control logic, or cryptographic downgrade code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited context shows ordinary invoice table labels, OCR language examples, and a PDF security heading. These do not support the scanner labels.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic code evaluation from CSV field
dangerous_patterns.description: The example uses eval() to parse invoice items from a CSV field. Safer alternatives include json.loads() or ast.literal_eval() with schema validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct eval() use on external data is visible at the cited line, with no sanitizer shown in the example.

content.value_statement: PDF work often requires many libraries and careful handling of text, tables, forms, and images. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical workflows for common PDF tasks.
actual_capabilities: Extract text from PDFs using pypdf and pdfplumber workflows.
Extract tables and save structured results for analysis.
Fill forms, merge documents, split pages, rotate pages, and add watermarks.
Create invoices, reports, and styled PDFs with ReportLab examples.
Manage PDF metadata, password protection, annotations, compression, and OCR workflows.
limitations: Requires Python libraries and system tools such as poppler or tesseract for some workflows.
OCR quality depends on scan resolution, language settings, and source document quality.
Complex table layouts may need manual tuning of extraction settings.
The examples should be reviewed before use with sensitive or untrusted documents.

use_cases.title: Extract Contract Data
use_cases.description: Pull text, tables, and metadata from PDF contracts for review or migration.

use_cases.title: Generate Business Documents
use_cases.description: Create invoices, reports, and formatted PDF packets from structured data.

use_cases.title: Prepare Document Pipelines
use_cases.description: Merge, split, watermark, compress, and OCR PDFs for repeatable workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Extract PDF Text
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner text extraction from one document.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PDF skill to extract readable text from this PDF. Preserve headings and page order.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Tables
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PDF skill to extract all tables from this report. Return cleaned table summaries and note any uncertain rows.

prompt_templates.title: Build a PDF Report
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a formatted report from supplied content.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PDF skill to design a ReportLab workflow for a monthly report with sections, charts, tables, and page numbers.

prompt_templates.title: Automate a Secure Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PDF skill to plan a batch pipeline that validates paths, extracts metadata, OCRs scans, merges outputs, and avoids unsafe eval patterns.

output_examples.input: Extract text and tables from a scanned supplier invoice.
output_examples.output: A page-by-page text summary, detected invoice table fields, OCR notes, and warnings for low-confidence values.

output_examples.input: Create a monthly PDF report from metrics and narrative sections.
output_examples.output: A structured report plan with cover page, executive summary, charts, tables, appendices, and library recommendations.

output_examples.input: Merge several PDFs, add a watermark, and update metadata.
output_examples.output: A safe workflow that validates input files, combines pages in order, applies the watermark, and records final metadata fields.

best_practices: Validate input and output paths before reading or writing PDF files.
Use json.loads or ast.literal_eval instead of eval when parsing structured data.
Test extraction settings on sample pages before processing a large batch.
anti_patterns: Do not run PDF workflows on untrusted documents without sandboxing and file limits.
Do not assume OCR output is correct without confidence checks or review.
Do not store passwords or sensitive metadata in examples, logs, or generated files.

faq.question: Which PDF tasks does this skill support?
faq.answer: It covers extraction, table parsing, forms, merging, splitting, creation, metadata, encryption, OCR, and optimization.

faq.question: Does it require extra software?
faq.answer: Some workflows need Python packages. OCR and image conversion can also require tesseract and poppler.

faq.question: Can it process scanned PDFs?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes OCR workflows using pdf2image and pytesseract, subject to scan quality.

faq.question: Can it create new PDFs?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes ReportLab workflows for invoices, reports, tables, images, and styled documents.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive PDFs?
faq.answer: Use caution. Validate paths, avoid logging sensitive data, and review metadata before sharing documents.

faq.question: What security issue was found?
faq.answer: One example uses eval on CSV data. Replace that pattern before using the example with untrusted input.
